sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Mr. Watson is an 86-year-old male who returns for follow-up for adult hydrocephalus.
He has a 8-10 year history of progressive gait impairment, cognitive impairment, and decreased bladder control.
He underwent shunt surgery on August 1st, 2020, with a Medtronic Strata programmable shunt in the ventricular peritoneal configuration set at level 2.0.
His symptoms have improved since surgery, with his walking ability increasing and he is now able to walk to the dining room without assistance, although he still requires help at times.
His bladder control has improved, but he now wears diapers.
His cognitive function has also improved, and he reports feeling less lost when conversing with others.
He has not experienced headaches, pain, or sores from wearing a diaper.
Past medical history: Adult hydrocephalus with a spino catheter protocol established in June 2020, shunt surgery on August 1st, 2020.
Review of systems: Gait impairment, urinary incontinence and urgency, cognitive impairment.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Subjective continued:
He is currently experiencing some improvement in his symptoms, but still requires assistance with walking and bladder control.
He has not had any recent hospitalizations.
Subjective continued:
His daughter, Theresa, reports that he has been doing much better since surgery, with improved walking ability and bladder control.
Subjective continued:

He is concerned about driving again, but the doctor informs him that it is unlikely that his movement speed will improve to the level needed to pass a driver safety evaluation, although it is possible that he could improve over a 9-12 month range.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/80, heart rate 64, respirations 18, weight 174 pounds, pain 0 out of 10.
Physical exam: The shunt site was clean, dry, and intact with a confirmed setting of 2.0.
He was able to converse easily and was cooperative during the exam.
Gait testing using the Tinetti assessment tool showed a score of 18-20 out of 28, slightly improved from his initial score.
Cranial nerves, pupils, and eoms were intact, with no disarchria or motor abnormalities.
Test results: CT scan from 10/15/2020 showed a frontal horn span at the level of foramen of Monro of 4.6 cm with a third ventricular contour that is flat with a span of 10 mm.
There was a tiny amount of blood in the right frontal region with a tiny subdermal collection, which was not noticed by the radiologist.
Assessment / Problem List:

Assessment: Mr. Watson is a pleasant and cooperative man who is experiencing some improvement in his symptoms of adult hydrocephalus, gait impairment, urinary incontinence and urgency, and cognitive impairment.
Problem list: Adult hydrocephalus; Gait impairment; Urinary incontinence and urgency; Cognitive impairment; Status: active.
Plan:
I will continue to monitor Mr. Watson's progress and will re-evaluate him in 3 months.
I will also recommend that he consider enrolling in a driver safety program to assess his ability to drive.
Follow-up: Mr. Watson will return in 3 months for re-evaluation.
